text: The surgeon identifies the portion of the small bowel to be resected and begins the procedure by entering the mesenteric border, similar to the initial step in an open surgery.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel at the mesenteric border of the mid-jejunum to create a window through to the opposite side, carefully managing small vessels to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon manipulates the small bowel over the energy device to assess the size and location of the created window. A linear stapling device is positioned through the window, and the surgeon uses their left hand to adjust the small bowel, ensuring it is properly aligned within the jaws of the stapler.

The surgeon positions the stapling device to angle away from the mesenteric border towards the anti-mesenteric border, ensuring optimal perfusion to the small bowel during resection. The technique involves passing the stapler through the mesenteric portion of the small bowel, opposite the specimen side.

The surgeon uses a linear stapling device, placing its tine through the mesenteric border of the small bowel. The device is articulated to achieve the correct angle for maintaining blood supply to the far corner opposite the specimen side.

The surgeon uses an energy device to transect the mesentery after the bowel has been transected on both sides, ensuring cauterization of smaller vessels along the mesenteric border to prevent bleeding. This technique is applied to maintain the blood supply to the specimen.
After the specimen is removed, the surgeon enters the anti-mesenteric corner of the staple line, which is visually identifiable as having the lowest blood supply.

The surgeon aligns the two sections of the small bowel adjacent to each other, preparing for closure. The smaller aneurotomy facilitates easier closure, and stay sutures may be used to ensure the bowel sections remain properly positioned for stapling.

The surgeon uses their left hand to position the small bowel across both tines of the stapler, ensuring alignment at the anti-mesenteric border. After removing the stapler, the staple line is checked, and preparations are made for suture repair.
The surgeon begins suturing with 2-0 Vicryl in a running fashion, starting at the apex of the bowel and continuing towards the staple line.
The surgeon continues suturing to completely close the aneurotomy, with the option to place an additional suture in a limber fashion to reinforce the suture line.

The surgeon ties the suture in place and addresses the mesenteric defect, which is larger in oncologic resections. The omentum is draped over the small bowel to restore its natural anatomical position.
